_note: Data-driven exercise library for the Sports at Home form-model engine. A figure is named joints (2-D coords in a 240x340 viewBox; the engine accepts an optional z per joint); a movement is keyframe poses the engine interpolates between, clocked by tempo. Object glyphs are referenced by id and drawn by web/js/formModel.js. Each entry also carries library metadata (pattern, tier, default/regression/progression objects, primary benefit, evidence refs, notes). Generated by scripts/normalize_incoming.py.

exercises.watch_for: Knees drifting inward — track them over your toes.
exercises.name: Supported Squat

cues.Down: Hold the chair; sit hips back and down
cues.Up: Drive through heels to stand tall

staticCues: Stand facing a sturdy chair and rest your hands lightly on its back.
Push your hips back as your knees bend, keeping most weight in your heels.
Use the chair only for balance, not to pull yourself up.
Stop at a depth that stays pain-free and controlled.
exercises.regression_object: shallower range / higher seat behind you
exercises.progression_lever: reduce hand support, then deepen range, then slow the lowering to 3-4s
exercises.primary_benefit: lower-body strength and confident sit-to-stand mechanics
exercises.notes: Joint-sparing entry squat; keep knees tracking over toes and breathe out as you stand. Stop for sharp knee or hip pain.

exercises.watch_for: Heels lifting — sit back as if reaching for a chair.
exercises.name: Bodyweight Squat

cues.Down: Hips back, chest tall, knees over toes

staticCues: Stand with feet about shoulder-width apart, arms reaching forward for balance.
Sit your hips back and down while keeping your chest up.
Lower to a depth you can control with heels flat.
Press the floor away to return to standing.
exercises.progression_lever: slow 3-4s eccentric, then deepen range, then add a loaded backpack
exercises.primary_benefit: foundational lower-body strength and mobility
exercises.notes: Keep weight mid-foot and knees in line with toes; exhale on the way up. Ease range if knees complain.

exercises.watch_for: Torso tipping forward — hug the pack tall against your chest.
exercises.name: Backpack Goblet Squat

cues.Down: Hug the pack to your chest; sink straight down

staticCues: Hold a loaded backpack against your chest like a goblet.
Squat straight down with an upright torso, elbows inside your knees.
Keep the pack close so your trunk stays braced.
Choose a load that leaves 2-3 reps in reserve at a moderate effort.
exercises.progression_lever: add reps, then a heavier pack, then a slow lowering tempo
exercises.primary_benefit: leg and trunk strength with an upright, knee-friendly squat
exercises.notes: Intensity by feel (RPE), not assumed kilograms; brace and exhale on the rise. Stop for sharp joint pain.

exercises.watch_for: Front knee wobbling — shorten the stance until it steadies.
exercises.name: Split Squat

cues.Down: Drop the back knee straight down

staticCues: Stand in a split stance with one foot forward and the other back (rear foot implied).
Lower by dropping the back knee toward the floor, torso upright.
Keep the front knee tracking over the front foot.
Hold a chair nearby for balance if needed; do all reps, then switch sides.
exercises.regression_object: hold a chair or wall for balance
exercises.progression_lever: add range, then a backpack, then a slow eccentric
exercises.primary_benefit: single-leg strength and balance with low joint load
exercises.notes: Per-leg work that also trains balance; keep the front shin fairly vertical. Reduce depth for knee discomfort.

exercises.watch_for: Dropping into the seat — lower with control for a slow count.
exercises.name: Single-Leg Sit-to-Stand

cues.Down: Lower under control to lightly tap the seat
cues.Up: Stand on one leg, arms forward

staticCues: Sit tall on a sturdy chair with one foot planted and the other lifted.
Stand up using mostly the planted leg, reaching your arms forward.
Lower slowly to tap the seat, then drive back up.
Use a higher seat or a light hand assist if the full rep is too hard.
exercises.progression_lever: lower the seat, then remove hand support, then slow the descent
exercises.primary_benefit: advanced single-leg strength and fall-resilient mechanics
exercises.notes: Demanding; keep a chair or wall within reach. Stop for sharp knee pain and regress the seat height.

exercises.watch_for: Losing the stick's three contact points — push the hips back, don't bow the spine.
exercises.name: Hip-Hinge Groove (Broomstick)

cues.Down: Push hips back; keep the stick on your spine

staticCues: Hold a broomstick along your spine, touching the back of your head, upper back, and tailbone.
Push your hips straight back with soft knees, letting your chest tip forward.
Keep all three contact points on the stick to groove a neutral spine.
Return by squeezing your glutes to bring the hips forward.
exercises.regression_object: hinge to a chair or wall behind you
exercises.progression_lever: increase range, then progress to a loaded RDL
exercises.primary_benefit: teaches a safe, neutral-spine hip hinge
exercises.notes: Movement-quality drill that protects the low back; keep the spine long, not rounded. Stop for back pain.

exercises.watch_for: Rounding the low back — soft knees, chest proud, hips travel backward.
exercises.name: Bodyweight Hip Hinge

cues.Down: Hips back, hands sliding down the shins

staticCues: Stand tall with soft knees and hands resting on the front of your thighs.
Hinge at the hips, sliding your hands down toward your shins.
Keep your back flat and feel a stretch in the hamstrings.
Drive the hips forward to return to standing.
exercises.progression_lever: add range, then load with a backpack RDL
exercises.primary_benefit: hamstring and glute strength with hinge skill
exercises.notes: Hinge from the hips, not the waist; keep a long spine and breathe steadily. Stop for back pain.

exercises.watch_for: Pack swinging away — keep it brushing your legs the whole way.
exercises.name: Backpack Romanian Deadlift

cues.Down: Hips back, pack close to the legs

staticCues: Hold a loaded backpack by its top handle in front of your thighs.
Hinge the hips back with nearly straight legs, lowering the pack along your legs.
Keep the pack close and your spine long throughout.
Stand by driving the hips forward; pick a load with 2-3 reps in reserve.
exercises.progression_lever: add reps, then a heavier pack, then a 3-4s lowering
exercises.primary_benefit: posterior-chain (hamstring and glute) strength
exercises.notes: Gauge effort by RPE, not kilograms; keep the load close and the back flat. Stop for low-back pain.

exercises.watch_for: Hips opening sideways — keep both hip points square to the floor.
exercises.name: Single-Leg Hinge

cues.Down: Tip forward as the free leg floats back

staticCues: Balance on one leg with a soft knee, the other leg ready to float behind you (implied).
Hinge forward at the hip while the free leg extends back like a seesaw.
Keep your hips level and your spine long.
Touch a wall or chair for balance; complete all reps, then switch legs.
exercises.regression_object: light fingertip support on a chair
exercises.progression_lever: remove support, then add range, then hold a light load
exercises.primary_benefit: single-leg hinge strength plus balance
exercises.notes: Combines posterior-chain strength with balance; keep hips square. Use support and stop for any back pain.

exercises.watch_for: Hips sagging — keep the bridge up while the heels slide.
exercises.name: Towel Hamstring Slider

cues.Down: Slide heels out, lowering the hips with control
cues.Up: Curl heels back, lift the hips

staticCues: Lie on your back on a smooth floor, heels on a towel, hips lifted into a bridge.
Slowly slide your heels away, letting your hips lower under control.
Curl the heels back in to lift the hips back to the bridge.
Reduce the slide distance if you feel hamstring cramping or strain.
exercises.default_object: bath towel on a smooth floor
exercises.progression_lever: increase slide range, then slow the eccentric, then add single-leg
exercises.primary_benefit: eccentric hamstring strength and bridge control
exercises.notes: Strong eccentric load on the hamstrings; ease range to avoid cramping. Stop for sharp hamstring or back pain.

exercises.watch_for: Head pecking forward — lead with the chest, body in one line.
exercises.name: Wall Push-Up

cues.Down: Bend the elbows; bring your chest toward the wall

staticCues: Stand arm's length from a wall with hands at shoulder height and width.
Bend your elbows to bring your chest toward the wall, body in one line.
Press firmly through your hands to return.
Step closer to the wall to make it easier, farther to make it harder.
exercises.progression_lever: step the feet back, then progress to a countertop
exercises.primary_benefit: entry-level pressing strength for chest, shoulders, triceps
exercises.notes: Easiest push-up regression; keep a straight line from head to heels and breathe out on the press. Stop for shoulder pain.

exercises.watch_for: Hips sagging — brace as one plank from head to heel.
exercises.name: Countertop Push-Up

cues.Down: Lower the chest toward the counter edge
cues.Up: Push back to a straight body

staticCues: Place your hands on a stable kitchen counter, shoulder-width apart.
Walk your feet back so your body forms a straight, angled line.
Bend the elbows to lower your chest toward the counter, then press up.
A higher surface is easier; a lower one is harder.
exercises.regression_object: use a higher, stable surface (e.g., wall)
exercises.progression_lever: lower the surface height, then move to an incline chair
exercises.primary_benefit: mid-range pressing strength on the path to floor push-ups
exercises.notes: Use a counter that will not slide or tip; keep the core braced and the body in one line. Stop for shoulder pain.

exercises.watch_for: Chair sliding — back it against a wall first.
exercises.name: Incline Push-Up (Chair)

cues.Down: Lower the chest to the seat edge
cues.Up: Press up to a straight line

staticCues: Place both hands on the seat of a sturdy, stable chair.
Walk your feet back into an angled plank with a straight body.
Bend the elbows to bring your chest to the seat, then press up.
Secure the chair against a wall so it cannot slide.
exercises.progression_lever: lower the surface, then progress to knee push-ups
exercises.primary_benefit: near-floor pressing strength with manageable load
exercises.notes: Brace the chair so it cannot slip; keep a straight body line and steady breathing. Stop for shoulder pain.

exercises.watch_for: Hips piking up — one straight line from knees to head.
exercises.name: Knee Push-Up

cues.Down: Lower the chest toward the floor

staticCues: Kneel on a mat with hands under your shoulders and knees behind your hips.
Keep a straight line from head to knees as you bend the elbows.
Lower your chest toward the floor, then press back up.
Place a cushion under the knees for comfort and shorten the range as needed.
exercises.progression_lever: increase range, then progress to a full push-up
exercises.primary_benefit: floor pressing strength with reduced load
exercises.notes: Keep hips from sagging by bracing the core; cushion the knees. Stop for shoulder or wrist pain.

exercises.watch_for: Elbows flaring wide — keep them about 45° from the body.
exercises.name: Full Push-Up

cues.Down: Lower with elbows about 45° from the body
cues.Up: Press the floor away, body in one line

staticCues: Start in a plank with hands under the shoulders and body in a straight line.
Bend the elbows to lower your chest toward the floor.
Press the floor away to return, keeping the hips level.
Drop to knees if your form breaks down before finishing the set.
exercises.progression_lever: add reps, then slow the lowering, then elevate the feet
exercises.primary_benefit: full-body pressing strength and trunk stability
exercises.notes: Keep a rigid line from head to heels and avoid flaring the elbows wide. Stop for shoulder pain and regress to knees.

exercises.watch_for: Holding the breath — press hard and keep breathing evenly.
exercises.name: Wall Press Hold

cues.Set: Lean in and set the hands at shoulder height
cues.Hold: Press steadily into the wall and hold

staticCues: Stand close to a wall with hands at shoulder height and a slight forward lean.
Press into the wall and hold a steady, sub-maximal effort.
Breathe normally throughout; never hold your breath.
Shorten the hold or reduce the lean if it feels too intense.
exercises.progression_lever: increase hold time, then lean in farther
exercises.primary_benefit: isometric pressing endurance for shoulders and chest
exercises.notes: Keep breathing during the hold; isometrics can raise blood pressure, so avoid breath-holding. Stop for shoulder pain or dizziness.

exercises.watch_for: Shrugging — draw the shoulder blades down and back, then pull.
exercises.name: Towel Isometric Row

cues.Set: Loop the towel and take up the slack
cues.Hold: Pull the elbow back and hold the squeeze

staticCues: Loop a towel around a fixed, secure anchor and hold an end in each hand.
Pull your elbows back and squeeze the shoulder blades, holding the position.
Keep your chest tall and breathe steadily during the hold.
Reduce the effort if you feel any neck or shoulder strain.
exercises.progression_lever: increase hold time, then add bent-row reps with a load
exercises.primary_benefit: isometric back and grip strength
exercises.notes: Anchor must be secure; keep breathing and the neck relaxed. Stop for shoulder or neck pain.

exercises.watch_for: Torso bouncing to swing the weight — the hinge stays still; the elbow draws past the ribs.
exercises.name: Water-Bottle Bent-Over Row

cues.Down: Let the weight hang, lengthening the arm

staticCues: Hinge forward with a flat back, a bottle or can in each hand hanging below you.
Row the weights toward your lower ribs, leading with the elbows.
Squeeze your shoulder blades, then lower under control.
Choose a load with 2-3 reps in reserve and keep the neck relaxed.
exercises.progression_lever: heavier bottles, then a slow lowering, then more reps
exercises.primary_benefit: upper-back and biceps strength to balance pushing
exercises.notes: Hinge from the hips and keep the spine long; effort by RPE not kilograms. Stop for low-back pain.

exercises.watch_for: Rounding the back — re-set the hinge before each pull.
exercises.name: Backpack Bent-Over Row

cues.Down: Lower the pack until the arms straighten
cues.Up: Row the pack to your waist

staticCues: Hinge forward with a flat back, holding a loaded backpack by its handle.
Row the pack toward your waist, driving the elbow up and back.
Pause briefly at the top, then lower with control.
Pick a load that keeps 2-3 reps in reserve at a moderate effort.
exercises.progression_lever: heavier pack, then more reps, then a slow eccentric
exercises.primary_benefit: back and arm pulling strength with a heavier load
exercises.notes: Keep a flat back and neutral neck; gauge load by feel. Stop for low-back or shoulder pain.

exercises.watch_for: Hips sagging — squeeze the glutes; test the table's sturdiness first.
exercises.name: Inverted Row (Sturdy Table)

cues.Down: Lower until the arms are straight
cues.Up: Pull the chest toward the edge

staticCues: Lie beneath a very sturdy table and grip the edge with both hands (horizontal pull shown).
Keep your body in a straight line and pull your chest toward the edge.
Lower yourself with control until the arms are straight.
Only use furniture rated to hold well above your bodyweight; bend the knees to make it easier.
exercises.regression_object: bend the knees / more upright body
exercises.progression_lever: straighten the body, then elevate the feet, then add reps
exercises.primary_benefit: advanced horizontal pulling strength for the whole back
exercises.notes: Test furniture stability and weight rating first; keep a rigid body and neutral neck. Stop for any joint pain.

exercises.watch_for: Neck craning — eyes to the floor, thumbs lead upward.
exercises.name: Prone Y-T-W Raise

cues.Down: Relax the arms toward the floor
cues.Up: Lift the arms overhead into a Y

staticCues: Lie face-down with your forehead resting and arms by your sides.
Lift your arms off the floor, sweeping them overhead into a Y shape.
Squeeze the mid-back; progress through T (arms out) and W (elbows bent) shapes over your sets.
Keep the movements small and controlled; light cans add resistance.
exercises.progression_lever: add light cans, then more reps, then longer holds at the top
exercises.primary_benefit: mid-back, rear-shoulder and posture strength
exercises.notes: Lift with the back muscles, not the neck; keep the movements small. Stop for shoulder or neck pain.

exercises.watch_for: Leaning away from the load — walk tall, as if between two walls.
exercises.name: Water-Bottle Farmer Carry

cues.StepL: March tall, weights pulling the shoulders down

staticCues: Hold a heavy bottle or can in each hand with arms straight at your sides.
Walk with tall posture and steady, even steps.
Let the load pull your shoulders down while you keep your ribs stacked.
Pick a weight you can carry for the time without your form breaking.
exercises.progression_lever: heavier load, then longer carries, then a single-side carry
exercises.primary_benefit: grip, core and postural endurance plus light conditioning
exercises.notes: Keep the trunk tall and braced; effort by feel. Set the load down if posture fails. Stop for back pain.

exercises.watch_for: Rushing — slow, deliberate knee lifts with the pack snug.
exercises.name: Backpack March

cues.StepL: March with a tall spine under the pack

staticCues: Wear a loaded backpack snug on both shoulders.
March in place or around the room with tall posture and active arms.
Keep your core braced and breathe in rhythm with your steps.
Adjust the pack weight so you can keep good posture the whole time.
exercises.progression_lever: heavier pack, then longer marches, then add a low step
exercises.primary_benefit: loaded postural endurance and light aerobic work
exercises.notes: Distribute the load on both shoulders and keep the spine tall. Stop for back pain or breathlessness.

exercises.watch_for: Hip jutting sideways — keep the pelvis level and ribs stacked.
exercises.name: Suitcase (Single-Side) Carry

cues.StepR: Stand tall, do not tip toward the load

staticCues: Hold one heavy bottle, can, or bag in a single hand at your side.
Walk tall while resisting the urge to lean toward the weight.
Keep your shoulders and hips level throughout.
Do the time on one side, then switch hands; choose a manageable load.
exercises.default_object: one water bottle, can, or bag
exercises.progression_lever: heavier load, then longer carries, then a longer reach overhead
exercises.primary_benefit: anti-lean core strength and grip endurance
exercises.notes: The goal is to stay upright against the side load; keep shoulders level. Stop for back or shoulder pain.

exercises.watch_for: Twisting from the low back — pivot the back foot and turn from the hips.
exercises.name: Bottle Wood-Chop

cues.Down: Chop diagonally from high to low across the body

staticCues: Hold a bottle or can in both hands up beside one shoulder.
Chop diagonally down and across toward the opposite hip, pivoting through the trunk.
Control the return back up to the start.
Do all reps on one diagonal, then switch sides; keep the load light to moderate.
exercises.progression_lever: heavier load, then faster controlled reps, then more range
exercises.primary_benefit: rotational core power and coordination
exercises.notes: Rotate through the trunk and hips, not the low back alone; keep it light. Stop for back pain.

exercises.watch_for: Torso turning toward the anchor — press slowly and stay square.
exercises.name: Anti-Rotation Press (Pallof)

cues.Set: Hold the towel at your chest, side anchor to one side
cues.Hold: Press straight out and resist the twist

staticCues: Loop a towel on a secure side anchor and hold both ends at your chest.
Press your hands straight out in front while resisting the sideways pull.
Keep your hips and shoulders square; breathe steadily.
Do the hold facing each direction; step closer to reduce the pull.
exercises.default_object: bath towel + a secure side anchor
exercises.progression_lever: step farther from the anchor, then longer holds
exercises.primary_benefit: anti-rotation core stability that protects the spine
exercises.notes: The point is to NOT rotate; keep breathing and the spine neutral. Stop for back pain.

exercises.watch_for: Gripping the support hard — lighten to fingertips as balance improves.
exercises.name: Supported Single-Leg Stand

cues.Set: Rest one hand on a chair and lift the far foot
cues.Hold: Balance on one leg and hold steady

staticCues: Stand next to a sturdy chair and rest one hand lightly on its back.
Lift the far foot off the floor and balance on the standing leg.
Keep your eyes forward and your standing knee soft.
Use less hand support over time; hold each side for equal time.
exercises.regression_object: two-hand support / lower the foot sooner
exercises.progression_lever: reduce hand support, then close the eyes briefly, then add a head turn
exercises.primary_benefit: single-leg balance to reduce fall risk
exercises.notes: Keep a chair within reach at all times; progress support gradually. Stop if you feel unsteady or dizzy.

exercises.watch_for: Staring at your feet — fix a point ahead at eye level.
exercises.name: Tandem (Heel-to-Toe) Stand

cues.Set: Place one foot directly in front of the other
cues.Hold: Hold the narrow stance, arms out for balance
cues.Release: Step back to a normal stance

staticCues: Stand with one foot directly in front of the other, heel to toe.
Hold the narrow stance with arms slightly out for balance.
Keep your gaze forward and weight even between both feet.
Stand beside a counter for safety and switch which foot leads.
exercises.progression_lever: narrow the base, then close the eyes briefly, then add arm movements
exercises.primary_benefit: static balance and ankle stability for fall prevention
exercises.notes: Practice near a counter you can grab; keep it safe. Stop if you feel unsteady or dizzy.

exercises.watch_for: Arms pinned to your sides — let them float out for balance, near a wall.
exercises.name: Heel-to-Toe Walk

cues.StepL: Place the heel just in front of the opposite toe

staticCues: Walk in a straight line placing each heel just in front of the other toe.
Keep your eyes forward and arms ready for balance.
Move slowly and deliberately, one careful step at a time.
Walk alongside a counter you can touch if you wobble.
exercises.progression_lever: narrow the line, then add head turns, then carry a light load
exercises.primary_benefit: dynamic gait balance to reduce falls
exercises.notes: Keep a wall or counter within reach; go slowly. Stop if you feel unsteady or dizzy.

exercises.watch_for: Pushing off the bottom foot — drive through the heel on the step.
exercises.name: Step-Up (Step or Sturdy Chair)

cues.Down: Step down with control, soft knee
cues.Up: Drive through the top foot to stand up

staticCues: Place one foot fully on a low, stable step or sturdy chair.
Drive through the top foot to stand up onto the step.
Step down slowly under control and repeat, then switch the lead leg.
Use a lower step and a handrail or wall for balance as needed.
exercises.progression_lever: raise the step height, then add a backpack, then slow the lowering
exercises.primary_benefit: single-leg strength, balance and confident stair mechanics
exercises.notes: Use a stable step that will not move and hold a rail if needed; lead with the working leg. Stop for knee pain.

exercises.watch_for: Holding the breath — march to a beat you could talk over.
exercises.name: Marching in Place

cues.StepL: Lift the knees and pump the arms

staticCues: March in place, lifting the knees and pumping the arms.
Find a rhythmic pace you can sustain while breathing comfortably.
Lift the knees higher or move faster to raise the intensity.
A great warm-up or short cardio snack between other movements.
exercises.regression_object: smaller, slower steps / hold a counter
exercises.progression_lever: lift the knees higher, then quicken the pace, then add arm reaches
exercises.primary_benefit: low-impact aerobic conditioning and warm-up
exercises.notes: Keep it comfortable and rhythmic; hold a counter if balance is an issue. Stop for chest pain or dizziness.

exercises.watch_for: Plopping down — control the descent on every rep.
exercises.name: Sit-to-Stand Intervals

cues.Down: Sit back to lightly touch the seat

staticCues: Sit tall on a sturdy chair with feet flat and hands forward.
Stand up briskly, then sit back down with control.
Repeat at a steady pace for a set time as a cardio snack.
Use a higher seat or slow the pace to make it easier.
exercises.progression_lever: quicken the pace, then add reps, then add a backpack
exercises.primary_benefit: aerobic conditioning plus repeated sit-to-stand power
exercises.notes: Keep knees tracking over toes and breathe through the reps. Stop for knee pain, chest pain, or dizziness.

exercises.watch_for: Skipping the handrail when tired — use it, and stop before wobbling.
exercises.name: Stair-Climbing Snack

cues.Down: Step back down with control
cues.Up: Climb the step at a brisk, steady pace

staticCues: Use a single stair or a low, stable step.
Step up and down at a brisk, steady pace for a short burst.
Hold the handrail for balance and let your breathing rise.
Slow the pace or shorten the burst to keep it comfortable.
exercises.progression_lever: quicken the pace, then lengthen the burst, then add a pack
exercises.primary_benefit: vigorous, time-efficient aerobic conditioning
exercises.notes: Use the handrail and a stable step; build pace gradually. Stop for chest pain, dizziness, or knee pain.

exercises.watch_for: Hips dropping — squeeze the glutes and tuck the ribs.
exercises.name: Knee Plank

cues.Set: Set up on the knees and forearms/hands
cues.Hold: Hold a straight line head to knees, brace and breathe

staticCues: Set up on your knees with hands or forearms under the shoulders.
Hold a straight line from head to knees, bracing your core.
Breathe steadily and keep the hips from sagging or piking.
Shorten the hold or rest as needed to keep good form.
exercises.progression_lever: increase hold time, then progress to a full plank
exercises.primary_benefit: core and trunk stability with reduced load
exercises.notes: Keep breathing during the hold; do not let the low back sag. Stop for low-back or shoulder pain.

exercises.watch_for: Head sagging — reach the crown long, one line heel to head.
exercises.name: Full Plank

cues.Set: Set up in a straight-arm or forearm plank
cues.Hold: Hold one rigid line head to heels and breathe

staticCues: Set up in a plank with hands (or forearms) under the shoulders and legs straight.
Hold one rigid line from head to heels, squeezing glutes and core.
Breathe steadily; never hold your breath during the hold.
Drop to a knee plank if your hips start to sag.
exercises.progression_lever: increase hold time, then elevate the feet, then add a reach
exercises.primary_benefit: whole-body trunk stability and endurance
exercises.notes: Keep breathing and a neutral spine; isometrics can raise blood pressure, so avoid breath-holding. Stop for back or shoulder pain.
